speaker: interviewee
text: I happened to know the concierge there, Mr. Toth, because I lived in a-- in the neighborhood, so. And I know her daughter, I always admire her daughter, who was a good friend of Zsa Zsa Gabor, you know, and all those things. She was a dancer, you know? And for young man, a dancer's always something to admire.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked Mr. Toth, what's going on here? And he said, listen, Les, I'll tell you something. The best thing for you to do, if you try to get out, because the Russian trucks come every couple hours and pick up all the people from the yard and take them to the railroad station, put them in cattle cars, and send them Russia as prisoners of war. You know? And I told this Wallenberg, I told him, what you going to do? You don't have any-- you have only one ID with you, and that's taken away. He said, I can-- I will talk to them. I will. I don't worry about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him, listen, I have a chance to go over the yard to another yard. There was approximately 8- to 10-foot-high brick wall separated the houses, you know, the yards. And on top of it, they had put like pieces of-- of glass and pieces of little steel thing, you know, so people won't climb over. And when he decided, he said, I'm a Swedish diplomat. I stay here. I mean, I wouldn't run away. After all, you know, it's-- I can tell them who I am. By the way, this was, anybody, the last time they seen him. And I decided, I am not a Swedish diplomat. I'd better go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Mr. Toth helped me. Well, he was-- we were watching out, that nobody, no other-- the Russians came to check back who there was there. He brought me a chair. And I-- I remember, the chair wasn't high enough. I have to put up on the arm of the chair, you know, in order for to go over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I grabbed the top, and then a piece of steel went into my hand, here, when I did, which I still have it here.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's going-- he's going to push in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I still have this piece of steel. It is right there, from this little escapade, you know? But this what's saved my life, because I was able to go across to the next yard, from the next yard to the next yard, and run back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told my aunt exactly what happened. You know? So I-- you know. And-- and that was the last time I seen Raoul Wallenberg. And-- and afterwards, you know that I went back to see if anything left from my father's place, back in Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Russians, they did a very thorough job. They-- in the house, our old house, they even took the window frames out. They even took the shingles from the roof and sent it back to Russia. I mean, there was absolutely like, like unbelievable. You know, I mean there was nothing, not a thing left, absolutely nothing, you know. And a very interesting thing that's come up, right now, I know my-- it maybe has nothing to do with the story, right now. But I just read in the paper, a couple days ago, that the Swiss banks tried to help people to get their money. But I don't know under what code name my dad put their money in. Because they put always money in, in a Swiss banks. But because of fear of persecution, you know, of a government, they used a code name.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I absolutely have no idea what code name my dad put money in the Swiss banks. You know? And it's-- it's-- it's interesting, you know, really. But the reason why I went back home, in case if I find something from my father, what left. But there was absolutely nothing. Absolutely nothing left you know, at all, nothing at all. And I went back. And I went to a school. And because my family were in the theater business before, part of the rest of the family, you know. And I, like I showed you the papers, you know? One of them showed I became a-- a director, you know, movie director.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the school, you know, all this. And also, I owned movie houses, you know, three movie houses, which I cannot own at the time, but I can rent from the government and run it. And that's what I did until December the 6th. This friend of mine, whose [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] brother I took out from the holding camp, who became a police lieutenant or captain, whatever, is in the Budapest police, came to me. And he told me this, I would warn you to leave the country, because we have an arrest warrant for you. Because I was too vehement to find out, you know, what happened to my father's holding and what's happened to the rest of our belongings, which the-- the communists, you know, took over. And he said, we have a-- an order for your arrest. So what happened, I just went over. I had a pass, which I showed you, you know, the railroad pass. And I used this railroad pass and went to the border, the Austrian border, because I had a movie right in a border city, in Sopron [NON-ENGLISH] was the name. It's a small movie theater. And I talked to the cleaner. And I told the cleaner-- I had to come up with a lie, because you don't trust nobody at the time. So I had-- I was afraid to carry a suitcase or anything with me. I had a little briefcase with me, with some of the papers I showed you. And I had a change of-- of underwear and extra socks, and that's it. And I told this man that I want to go to Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I want to smuggle some medicine, because, at the time, there was no medicine. And if you help me, I will give you all-- half of the stuff if you wait for me and bring me back. And as far as I know, he's still waiting for me to go back, [LAUGHS] you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But the point is that that's the only that I could go through the border. And I went from-- the first city I went was Wiener Neustadt. And that's-- I took the overnight train, from Wiener Neustadt, and I went over to Vienna. And I ended up in the American sector in Vienna. But I didn't want to stay there, because that's, I felt, too close. You know, because this was a Russian sector. They had a French sector, English, and American sector.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wanted to go to Germany, to the German sector-- I mean, in Germany, because the southern Germany was occupied strictly by the United States Army. So what happened is this, you know. I had a coat, an overcoat. I exchanged an overcoat for a passport of a woman. And they doctored up the picture, almost like looked like me. And because when we go from there to Salzburg, they had to go through a Russian checkpoint, because there from the Russian zone, you went to them on the-- the American zone. Salzburg was an American zone. So in order to buy this, I gave up my coat, in December, to get over to Salzburg. And from Salzburg, again, for the last money I had, I gave for a guy to took me over to Germany, to the German and the Austrian border.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I ended up in Bamberg, Germany, and I-- as a refugee, as a Hungarian refugee. And I find a job with the United Nations Children's service, UNESCO. And I work with the-- their job was, at the time, was different like today. Their job was to reunite children with parents who were lost. And I worked with an English lady, by the name of Huxley. And Agnes Huxley was her name. And I worked with her and until I came to the United States in 1951. And I came to Buda-- to-- to Pittsburgh. And I received a scholarship to the University of Pittsburgh. And I-- my first job was as I-- part of my-- my study was I had to work on the educational station for credit. And a first job was I worked with Fred Rogers, on which, at the time, they called And at the time, there was only two television stations in Pittsburgh. One was KDKA, which at the time called WDTV-TV, and channel 13, WQED. And the people came in to open another television station, an NBC station. And they had no television background. So they came to WQED to learn something television. And I worked with them. And they get to know me. And when they'd opened the station, they offered me a job. So I went there and worked with them. And the next year, the Hearst Corporation opened a station there, WTAE-Television. They offered me a job with more money, so I worked there as a television, news, and documentary photographer. And afterwards, I was exclusively with them a sports-- sports photographer for WTAE-Television. Then, in 1969, you know, '68, '69, I got a call from the Pittsburgh Steeler's President, Dan Rooney, want to know if I want to work for the Pittsburgh Steelers. He said, I just hired a new coach, Chuck Noll, and we would like to have if you be our coaching photographer. And the same day, I got a call from Joe L. Brown, who was, at the time, the general manager of the Pittsburgh Pirates. He said, Les, I want you to work for us. I told him, Joe, I just got a call from Danny Rooney. He said, I know. I talked to them. You can work for both of us. We make arrangement.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I worked for them until 1976, I worked for the Pirates and Steelers. In fact, here is a World Series ring that prove it. This, the 1971 World Series ring, which I earned with the Pirates, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and-- and in 1976, you know, when Jackie Sherrill became the head coach of the Pittsburgh, University of Pittsburgh football team, he heard about me. And he took-- and because I covered-- met-- you know, had a lot of friends in this organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me, Les, how about you work for me? I-- this-- I told him, I don't know. And my family wanted me to change, because I travelled too much between the two teams, especially July, August, September, October, you know? Because both of them in a playoff position. So I went, and I worked for the University of Pittsburgh ever since. And I went through the football coaches of Jackie Sherrill and Foge Fazio and Mike Gottfried and Hackett. And right now, I working with Johnny Majors. He's my coach. So I went through all these people. So I believe, I proved that more than once that I'm a survivor. [LAUGHS] I know you've taken us to the end. But I want to go back. I have some questions for you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, ma'am.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that would like to talk about.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry if I forget something, but I get sometimes carried away.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, don't worry. First, I need-- I need for you to explain to us who Wiedemayer is, the German, the man whose name you signed. Why don't you tell us a little bit about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you start by talking about-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Wiedemayer was in charge of the Eastern SS, Waffen-SS group, the nationality SS group, not the German, so-called German-German SS. He was in charge of the different like the Ukrainian SS, the Lithuanian SS, the Croatian SS. He was in charge of the Eastern SS groups. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was the commander of this group. And he had absolute power, you know, of-- of whatever was going. Like, for instance, one day, we needed 10 cars, because some of the cars, they went close to the front line, and they were ruined. He sent me out with nine SS men to a private garage, where the people kept their cars in a big apartment house. He said, I want you to pick up 10 garage. Who would resist, you know, 10 SS men with machine guns? Anything what they needed, you know? Anything what we need. What I forgot to say this, and which I did not mention, and I'm glad just we go back to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: When, in the very first time, in 1944, when I was in the headquarters, trains used to come in with different supplies. And they came to the railroad yard, you know, where they were sending them for different directions. And when they arrived, they had to go around and put on a railroad car the direction, which way they should be sent, you know, by the railroad workers. You know, like they were like-- I don't know what the name of it here. It's like a-- a-- a central point, where you-- the car goes there, and you can send them to different directions.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I find out exactly when the train coming, when they're supposed to go. So we had people, for the underground made up papers, and, for instance, all the ammunitions, all the supplies, which supposed to go to the East, we sent them to Italy. And [LAUGHS] all the supplies, like we sent in-- in wintertime, we sent their supplies, which supposed to go to Italy-- shorts, you know, and-- and heavy oil and ammunition-- we sent them to the East Front, you know? So they're suddenly, instead of having their winter uniform, they receive shorts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- and the German Army, in the Italian campaign, suddenly, instead of shorts, they received heavy, winter uniforms. I mean, it's-- it's the little things, you know, really, but we felt whatever we can do to help the war effort, for the Allies, you know, naturally we did. This was part of our job. Or for instance, one time, one of the underground men, whom I gave a German uniform, at the time, because certain places I could not go alone, naturally. But he wasn't never in a camp, because I-- you know, he wasn't officially like I was. You know, I could go anywhere. And we-- we were going to pick up some people from an island, and some woman, which later on I did pick up. And we seen, in a corner, the people was beating up, badly, a person.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stopped the car, because we seen the person, you know, had no-- you know, a civilian, but had no star, nothing. We didn't know if he was Jewish or not. And when we go down, two Germans get up. And so I, purposely, since I, you know, spoke Hungarian and everything, I told them, what's going on? What are you doing with this person? Oh, we recognize him. He's a Jew. He didn't wear a star. He tried to get out. Oh, I told him, don't worry. We take care of him. Let's give him to us. He said, what are you going to do with him? I tell him. I put the-- you know, I had the luger that-- you-- you know what we do with these people like this? But not in the street. You know, I put him back. The poor kid was crying. He said, please don't kill me. Don't kill me. So I told him, hey, listen. You don't have to worry about us. We're trying to save you. Tell me where would be the safest place for you, where we can let you go? First of all, I told him, clean yourself a little bit, because he looked bloody and everything. And he told me where we can let him go, when he felt this was safe at friend's. So we let him go there. Then we went to the island, you know, where this-- well, not I-- what they call it? Like not warehouse. Like a marketplace, where they kept women, you know, who's supposed to be deported next day. And they were Hungarian guards there, you know? So I walked in with my paper. And the officers salute me, you know, heil Hitler and everything. I told them, I am here. We taking over these woman, because we're going to deport them tomorrow morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he said, they said we should stay here until they come after the-- We are here early. You guys can go. Oh, fine. So this was early in the afternoon. We waited until it was dark. And we hold all the women together. And I told them, now listen, I have something to tell you. But if anybody scream or anybody show any kind of emotion loud, I use that machine gun, and I shoot every one of you. And I told them, we are in the-- from the underground. We are here to save you not to be deported. And start it-- ssh. Then listen, I shoot, because you are endangering the rest of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they start, oh, oh. They were so happy, you know? I told them, I will, soon as it's dark enough, I let you go, two or three at a time. I have no place for you to do, because I had no more room to put any more people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I let you out. And you have to find a place for yourself somewhere. And by midnight, they were all gone. All the women, they were gone. I-- I mean, it's-- we try to do whatever we can to help people whom we can help. And but the same time, it's a very thankless task, sometime. Because I have a lady, her name was Mrs. Harsanyi. I saved one of her son. And I saved one of her daughter, who, right now, lives in Israeli if she's still alive. And after the war, the same lady looked at, in my face, and she told me, you killed my son, because I could not save the other son, because she told me where she was. And when I went there, they was already moved away, you know, the-- the camp. And this woman, whom I saved two of her children, because I could not save the third one, she told me, right in my face, you killed my son. Now you know how I felt.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had certain downside of it, too, you know. And my-- my worst nightmare was when I find out, after the war, from a person who told me that, Les, I lost my wife, because I didn't have money to buy the paper from Mr. Schweikert. I told him, what? I gave the paper to give it to the people. I risked my life to save people. I risked everything. For every time I saved a person, I risked my own life. When I gave a paper away, you know, if they would have come, it was the same way. I did not realize there were people who profited by, instead of giving it, they try to sell. Jewish people were selling it to Jewish people, try to sell the paper, which was-- I was very much appalled with, because this happened, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I find out, after the war, naturally, because when this man told me this, his wife died because they could not come up with enough money to buy the paper, which I gave them to give it to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So life is-- how can I explain to you? People do certain things because believe in it. And certain people think of themselves, only, and profit. And sometime you kind of lose faith in human nature when you heard things like this happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my-- the only survivors I have, right now, is my cousin, Dr. Markovich, George, whose address I have somewhere for you. He-- he is a surgeon. He was the head of-- of Margaret Hospital in Budapest after the war. My cousin, Susan-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the man in Pittsburgh, who would not cooperate for a simple reason, because he don't want to admit that he's Jewish. And he was in the hole, but he married a Catholic girl. And the family think he is Catholic. And he don't want to blow his cover. And I told him, a couple of times, George, I think, by saving your life, I would deserve-- because I looking for you. And by they way, the most interesting story, how I find these people, here, in Pittsburgh. It-- it's fate has-- has 1,000 stories. I-- when I went to school at Pitt, I worked at Sears for a while. And I became good friends with an optometrist by the name of Dr. Rothschild. And we used to go out for lunch together. And one day, I got a phone call from-- as I am in the-- I am at Sears.

speaker: interviewee
text: He calls me from his optometric office. And he says, Les, could you come and help me out? He said, I have a couple here, and I-- I cannot make heads or tails of it what they want. I walk into this office, and George will tell you, this guy will tell you this much. I walk into this office, and I don't recognize. I mean, I-- my god, this was 30 years after, over 20 years? No, actually, it wasn't 20 years. It was in 1958, but 14 years after. People change, naturally. And I-- unless people you think, you know, of run into. He says to me, would you talk to the people, ask them what they want? So I ask him, what language do you speak? I talk to them German. And they start to speak this broken German. Then I said, what other language you speak? And I-- she said, Hungarian. Oh, I thought, incredible, wow, Hungarian. And this moment, the lady jump in me, said, oh, Les. She said, you saved my life. You saved my husband's life. You saved my son's life. And I find these people. And I looked for them, for 10 years, all over the world. And by accidentally, by pure accident-- I mean, you know, fiction-- how can I-- truth is-- is stranger than fiction. You cannot invent a story like this, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And George Taylor, who lives in Pittsburgh, if you ask him, he can tell you the story, because that's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you a little bit more about this. How did you-- where did you sleep every night? And how did people not recognize you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- because I was in the SS uniform. The people-- you know, you put the uniform on, you don't look the same. And I never-- I made sure, I never came together with people. You know, only at night, I went to the place. I did not go daytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was funny, after the war, the people right across pointed out to me, said, hey, didn't we see this guy in German uniform? Right across the street, after the war! [LAUGHS] But during the war, I went always at night. And I had the German uniform on, you know? And-- and-- and people just-- I made sure, I did not come together with people, you know, who recognize me, except at one time. The guy by the name of Ignac, he was my classmate in Hungary. You know? And he joined the same SS group as I did, but under his own name. And he recognized me. And he never said a word. Never said a word. I don't know if he was quite sure, because I was under a different name. He know me as Banos Laszlo. And I was George Nemeth. OK? So after the war, I seen this man on the street. And when he see me, he started to run. And I went after him. I told him, Ignac, you don't have to worry. A life for a life. I said, did you recognize me?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, yes, I thought it was you, but I wasn't sure. I told him, you know, one word from you, and I would have been shot there on sight. I never told anybody this, he was in the SS. I could have put him in away from life, but he saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] What--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know it's unbelievable, you know. Because-- that's a main reason-- that's a main reason, you know, for a long, long time, I did not want to say this, because, until I went to Hungary the first time, in 1982 or '83, and I find these people still alive, you know. And I know who can testify that's what happened. And certainly, it sounds like, you know, like a fiction, you know, written for television. I had to wait until I find people who-- who can testify, in my behalf, what I am saying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the funny part is this. You know, I showed you the tape, this hole? It's another one, right above it, which is not open, because people don't know this was still there, because they-- we covered it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for some reason, this man, who came, he didn't know about the hole above. And this one is still can be opened. That place still exists the same way as it was in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why don't you tell me what happened to your father and to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and my brother, they were also in the underground. And you know, it's a sad story, when you think of it, you know. Because I went there and visited them. I gave them all the papers and everything what they needed in order to save them. But I was out to deliver two fliers to the partisans, because I had to do it. And because of this, this cost my father's and my brother's life.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to--
